locations.description: The great harbour city of ancient Egypt, seat of the Mouseion and its daughter library — a cosmopolitan crossroads where scrolls arrived with every merchant ship
The House of Wisdom in ninth-century Baghdad, a palace annex of translation halls, observatories, and map rooms humming with the scratch of reed pens
The monastic university complex in Bihar, its red-brick towers visible for miles, home to nine million volumes before the fires came
Philip II's austere granite monastery-palace outside Madrid, whose library vaults held forbidden manuscripts alongside royal correspondence
The refounded Oxford library of 1602, its Jacobean reading rooms kept deliberately unheated so readers would not linger too long
The Capitol building's north wing in early-nineteenth-century Washington, whose small but symbolically charged collection was ash by 1814 and rebuilt from Jefferson's personal shelves

hard_facts: The Library of Alexandria likely declined gradually through budget cuts and neglect rather than a single fire
The Bayt al-Hikma translation movement outlasted the Mongol sack through scholars who had already dispersed
Nalanda's destruction in 1193 is documented by Tibetan sources; some monks carried texts to Tibet
threads.label: The paradox of preservation
threads.resolution: Every library that survived did so by accident, compromise, or migration — the deliberate act of preservation alone has never been enough; the series closes on a digital-archive librarian who grasps this fully

hard_facts: Ptolemy III allegedly detained travellers to copy their scrolls
Al-Mamun's translation programme was partly motivated by astrological statecraft
Jefferson sold his library to Congress at a personal profit while the nation was in debt
threads.label: Knowledge as political object
threads.resolution: Each institution reflects the ambitions of its patron — imperial, caliphal, royal, republican — and the series traces how that entanglement both funds and endangers the books

hard_facts: Women were formally excluded from the Bodleian until 1877
Nalanda accepted students from across Asia regardless of caste, an unusual policy for its era
The Library of Congress reading room opened to the public in 1800 but was destroyed before most could use it
threads.label: Who is allowed inside
threads.resolution: Access has always been the hidden argument — the series ends with open-access movements as the latest chapter in a very old dispute, unresolved
